activities.title: Masters of Disguise: The Astonishing Octopus
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about octopuses, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: Masters of Disguise: The Astonishing Octopus
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: The octopus is one of the most extraordinary
creatures in the ocean. Found in every sea on
Earth, from shallow tropical reefs to the cold,
crushing depths, these remarkable animals have
fascinated scientists for centuries. Although they
are sometimes mistaken for fish, octopuses are in
fact molluscs, closely related to snails, slugs and
cuttlefish. What sets them apart, however, is a
combination of intelligence and adaptability that is
almost unmatched in the animal kingdom.
Perhaps the octopus's most famous talent is its
ability to vanish in plain sight. Beneath its skin
lie millions of tiny, colour-filled sacs called
chromatophores. By expanding or contracting these
sacs, an octopus can change its colour in less than
a second, blending seamlessly into rocks, coral or
sand. This ingenious camouflage allows the octopus
to evade hungry predators and to ambush
unsuspecting prey before they realise the danger.
Octopuses are widely regarded as the most
intelligent of all invertebrates — animals without
a backbone. In laboratories, they have learned to
open screw-top jars to reach a tasty crab inside,
and some have been seen using coconut shells as
portable shelters. Remarkably, around two-thirds
of an octopus's neurons are found not in its head
but in its eight arms, which can taste, touch and
explore quite independently of the brain itself.
The body of an octopus is wonderfully strange. It
has three hearts that pump blue, copper-rich blood,
and not a single bone anywhere inside it. Because
it is boneless, an octopus can squeeze through any
gap larger than its beak, the only hard part of its
body. Scientists have watched a heavy octopus pour
itself through a hole no wider than a small coin,
escaping from tanks that seemed completely sealed.
When camouflage fails, the octopus has other tricks.
Many species release a cloud of dark ink to confuse
an attacker, then escape by jet propulsion, squirting
water to shoot backwards at speed. If a predator
seizes an arm, the octopus can shed it and grow a
new one later, a process known as regeneration. The
aptly named mimic octopus goes further still,
contorting its body to impersonate more dangerous
creatures such as venomous sea snakes and lionfish.
For all their gifts, octopuses live surprisingly brief
lives, usually only one or two years. Females guard
their eggs with extraordinary devotion, often refusing
to eat for months until the eggs hatch, after which
they usually die. Today, engineers studying these
elusive animals look to their boneless arms when
designing soft, flexible robots, proving that the
octopus still has many secrets left to teach us about
the hidden workings of the natural world.

What sets them apart, however, is a combination of intelligence and adaptability that is almost unmatched in the animal kingdom.

By expanding or contracting these sacs, an octopus can change its colour in less than a second, blending seamlessly into rocks, coral or sand. This ingenious camouflage allows the octopus to evade hungry predators and to ambush unsuspecting prey before they realise the danger.

Octopuses are widely regarded as the most intelligent of all invertebrates — animals without a backbone. In laboratories, they have learned to open screw-top jars to reach a tasty crab inside, and some have been seen using coconut shells as portable shelters. Remarkably, around two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are found not in its head but in its eight arms, which can taste, touch and explore quite independently of the brain itself.

The body of an octopus is wonderfully strange. It has three hearts that pump blue, copper-rich blood, and not a single bone anywhere inside it. Because it is boneless, an octopus can squeeze through any gap larger than its beak, the only hard part of its body. Scientists have watched a heavy octopus pour itself through a hole no wider than a small coin, escaping from tanks that seemed completely sealed.

When camouflage fails, the octopus has other tricks. Many species release a cloud of dark ink to confuse an attacker, then escape by jet propulsion, squirting water to shoot backwards at speed. If a predator seizes an arm, the octopus can shed it and grow a new one later, a process known as regeneration. Females guard their eggs with extraordinary devotion, often refusing to eat for months until the eggs hatch, after which they usually die. options: fish such as cod and herring.
questions.explanation: The text says octopuses are 'in fact molluscs, closely related to snails, slugs and cuttlefish'. They are only mistaken for fish.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "ingenious" (line 18)?

questions.prompt: Why is an octopus able to squeeze through very small gaps?
options: Because it has no bones in its body.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements is TRUE?
options: About two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms.
Octopuses are a type of fish.
Most of an octopus's neurons are in its head.
Octopuses are unable to change colour.
questions.answer: About two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms.
questions.explanation: The text states 'around two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are found not in its head but in its eight arms'. The other options all contradict the passage.

questions.prompt: In the text, "regeneration" (line 46) refers to the octopus's ability to:
questions.explanation: The text describes how an octopus can 'shed it and grow a new one later, a process known as regeneration'.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "vanish in plain sight" (lines 12–13) mean?
options: To become almost invisible while staying where it is, using camouflage.
To disappear completely from the ocean.
To swim away as fast as possible.
To hide inside a coconut shell.
To turn into a completely different animal.
questions.answer: To become almost invisible while staying where it is, using camouflage.
questions.explanation: It means blending in so well that it cannot be seen even though it has not actually moved away — that is camouflage.

questions.prompt: In "these remarkable animals" (line 4), what type of word is "remarkable"?
questions.explanation: 'Remarkable' describes the noun 'animals', so it is an adjective.

activities.title: The Busy World of Honeybees
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about honeybees, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Busy World of Honeybees
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Inside a single beehive, tens of thousands of
honeybees live and work together in remarkable
harmony. To an outsider it may look like chaos,
yet every bee has a clear role to play. A hive is
really a busy city, humming day and night, where
each insect labours for the good of the whole
colony rather than for itself.
Most of the bees in a hive are female workers.
These tireless insects clean the cells, feed the
young, guard the entrance and gather food. The
single queen has just one vital task: to lay the
eggs that keep the colony alive, sometimes more
than a thousand in a single day. The male bees,
called drones, are larger and lazier, and their
only job is to mate with a new queen. When food
grows scarce, the drones are often driven out.
To make honey, worker bees fly from flower to
flower, sipping a sugary liquid called nectar. They
store it in a special honey stomach and carry it
back to the hive. There the nectar is passed from
bee to bee and fanned with beating wings until the
watery part evaporates. What remains is thick,
golden honey, sealed inside wax cells to feed the
colony through the long, cold winter months.
Finding flowers is only half the battle, for a bee
must also tell the others where the food lies. A
returning worker performs a curious wiggling
movement known as the waggle dance. The angle
of the dance reveals the direction of the flowers,
while the length of each waggle hints at how far
the hungry bees must travel. In this clever way,
a single scout can guide hundreds of companions.
Honeybees matter far beyond their hives. As they
move between blossoms, they carry pollen from
one plant to another, allowing fruits and seeds to
form. This process, called pollination, helps to
produce a huge share of the crops we eat, from
apples and almonds to beans and berries. Without
these tiny labourers, our plates would be far
emptier and our countryside far less colourful.
Sadly, honeybees now face serious threats. The
loss of wild meadows, the spread of disease and
the careless use of chemical sprays have all made
their lives harder. Some colonies have collapsed
altogether, leaving empty hives behind. Scientists
and farmers are working to protect these vital
insects, because a world without bees would be a
poorer and far hungrier place for everyone.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what is the queen bee's vital task?
options: To guard the entrance to the hive.
To gather nectar from the flowers.
To lay the eggs that keep the colony alive.
To mate with the male drones.
To fan the nectar with her wings.
questions.answer: To lay the eggs that keep the colony alive.
questions.explanation: The text says the queen has 'just one vital task: to lay the eggs that keep the colony alive'.

questions.prompt: What is the only job of the male bees, called drones?
options: To mate with a new queen.
To make honey for the winter.
questions.answer: To mate with a new queen.
questions.explanation: The passage states that the drones' 'only job is to mate with a new queen'.

questions.prompt: How is the watery part of the nectar removed inside the hive?
options: It is left out in the sunshine to dry.
It is fanned with beating wings until it evaporates.
It is poured straight into the wax cells.
It is carried away by the drones.
It is swallowed by the queen bee.
questions.answer: It is fanned with beating wings until it evaporates.
questions.explanation: The nectar is 'fanned with beating wings until the watery part evaporates', leaving thick honey behind.

questions.prompt: The phrase that a hive is 'really a busy city' suggests that the writer wants us to think bees are:
options: lonely creatures that avoid each other.
questions.explanation: Comparing the hive to a busy city full of bees that 'labour for the good of the whole colony' shows they are organised and hard-working.

questions.prompt: Why does the writer call the drones 'larger and lazier' than the workers?
options: Because drones do far less work than the busy female workers.
Because drones are the rulers of the whole hive.
Because drones make most of the honey.
Because drones are too small to be seen.
Because drones lay all of the eggs.
questions.answer: Because drones do far less work than the busy female workers.
questions.explanation: The workers do many jobs, while a drone's 'only job is to mate', so the writer pictures drones as doing much less work.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements is TRUE?
options: Drones do most of the work inside the hive.
Bees carry pollen between plants, which is called pollination.
Honey is made from the sugary liquid in tree bark.
Every bee in the hive works only for itself.
The queen gathers nectar from the flowers.
questions.answer: Bees carry pollen between plants, which is called pollination.
questions.explanation: The text explains that bees 'carry pollen from one plant to another', and that 'this process' is 'called pollination'. The other options all contradict the passage.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "scarce" (line 16)?
questions.explanation: 'Scarce' means hard to find or in short supply, so 'rare' is the closest match.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "evaporates" (line 24)?
questions.explanation: When the watery part 'evaporates' it turns into vapour and goes into the air, leaving thicker honey behind.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "our plates would be far emptier" (lines 43–44) suggest?
options: Bees would steal the food from our kitchens.
We would have to buy bigger plates for our meals.
We would have much less food to eat without bees.
Honey would be the only food left for people.
Our plates would be cleaner and easier to wash.
questions.answer: We would have much less food to eat without bees.
questions.explanation: Because bees pollinate 'a huge share of the crops we eat', losing them would mean far less food on our plates.

questions.prompt: In "These tireless insects clean the cells" (lines 9–10), what type of word is "clean"?
questions.explanation: Here 'clean' is the action that the insects do to the cells, so it is a verb.

vocabulary.meaning: a peaceful state where things work well together
a large group of animals or insects living together
working very hard without seeming to get tired
extremely important or necessary
hard to find or not enough to go round
the sweet, sugary liquid that bees collect from flowers
turns from a liquid into a vapour and goes into the air
one who goes ahead to explore and bring back information
friends or others who stay and travel together
the moving of pollen between plants so seeds and fruit can form

activities.title: Inside a Volcano
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about how volcanoes form and erupt, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: Inside a Volcano
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Few sights in nature are as terrifying or as
magnificent as an erupting volcano. To understand
how one forms, we must journey deep beneath our
feet. The surface of the Earth is not a single,
solid shell but is broken into enormous slabs of
rock called tectonic plates. These plates drift
very slowly, carried along by the hot, churning
rock that lies far below them. Most volcanoes are
born where two of these plates meet, pull apart
or grind painfully against one another.
Beneath the ground, intense heat and pressure melt
rock into a thick, glowing liquid. While this molten
rock remains trapped underground, scientists call
it magma. When it finally bursts through the surface
during an eruption, the very same material is given
a new name: lava. As the lava cools in the open
air, it hardens once more into solid rock, slowly
building the towering, cone-shaped mountains that
we recognise as volcanoes the world over.
Not every eruption behaves in the same way. Some
volcanoes ooze rivers of runny lava that flow
gently downhill, giving people time to move away
to safety. Others are violently explosive. When
the magma is thick and sticky, trapped gases cannot
escape easily and build up enormous pressure, until
the volcano bursts apart in a single devastating
blast. Such eruptions can hurl ash, rock and choking
fumes high into the sky, blotting out the sun for
miles around and burying whole towns below.
History remembers many famous volcanoes. In the
year 79, Mount Vesuvius buried the Roman city of
Pompeii beneath metres of ash, preserving it for
centuries. Mount Etna in Italy still rumbles today,
while Japan’s graceful Mount Fuji has not erupted
for over three hundred years. Each one reminds us
of the tremendous forces hidden quietly beneath the
calm surface of our planet.
Today, scientists called volcanologists watch these
restless mountains closely. They measure tiny
earthquakes, sniff for escaping gases and track the
slightest swelling of the ground, all of which can
warn that magma is rising. Although no one can
predict an eruption perfectly, these careful clues
allow experts to evacuate people before disaster
strikes, saving countless lives.
Yet volcanoes are not purely destructive. The ash
they scatter is rich in minerals, and over many
years it breaks down into wonderfully fertile soil.
Farmers therefore plant crops on volcanic slopes,
and millions of people choose to live in their
shadow. In this strange way, the very mountains
that can destroy life also help to nourish it.

questions.prompt: What are the enormous slabs of rock that make up the Earth’s surface called?
questions.explanation: The passage says the surface of the Earth is broken into enormous slabs of rock called tectonic plates.

questions.prompt: According to the text, where are most volcanoes born?
options: In the centre of a tectonic plate.
Where two plates meet, pull apart or grind together.
High above the clouds in the sky.
questions.answer: Where two plates meet, pull apart or grind together.
questions.explanation: The passage states that most volcanoes are born where two plates meet, pull apart or grind against one another.

questions.prompt: What happens to lava as it cools in the open air?
options: It hardens once more into solid rock.
It becomes fertile soil at once.
questions.answer: It hardens once more into solid rock.
questions.explanation: The text explains that as the lava cools in the open air it hardens once more into solid rock.

questions.prompt: Why does the passage suggest that runny lava is less dangerous than explosive eruptions?
options: Because runny lava is much hotter than thick lava.
Because it flows gently and gives people time to move to safety.
Because it never reaches the surface at all.
Because it creates better soil for farmers.
Because volcanologists can always stop it.
questions.answer: Because it flows gently and gives people time to move to safety.
questions.explanation: The text says runny lava flows gently downhill, giving people time to move away to safety, which makes it less deadly.

questions.prompt: Why does thick, sticky magma tend to cause a more violent eruption?
options: Because the trapped gases cannot escape and pressure builds up.
Because it cools far too quickly to flow.
Because it contains no minerals at all.
Because it is found only near Mount Fuji.
Because volcanologists make it explode on purpose.
questions.answer: Because the trapped gases cannot escape and pressure builds up.
questions.explanation: When the magma is thick and sticky, trapped gases cannot escape and build up enormous pressure until the volcano bursts apart.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements is TRUE?
options: Mount Fuji erupts every single year.
Magma and lava are made of completely different rock.
Volcanic ash makes the surrounding soil less fertile.
Mount Vesuvius buried the city of Pompeii in ash.
questions.answer: Mount Vesuvius buried the city of Pompeii in ash.
questions.explanation: The text states that in the year 79 Mount Vesuvius buried the Roman city of Pompeii beneath metres of ash. The other options all contradict the passage.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "molten" (line 13)?
questions.explanation: ‘Molten’ describes rock that has been melted by heat into a glowing liquid, so ‘melted’ is the closest match.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "evacuate" (line 47)?
options: to move people away from danger.
questions.answer: to move people away from danger.
questions.explanation: To ‘evacuate’ people means to move them away from a dangerous place before disaster strikes.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "blotting out the sun" (lines 28–29) suggest about an explosive eruption?
options: That so much ash fills the sky that the sunlight is hidden.
That the eruption only happens during the night.
That the lava is brighter than the sun itself.
That the volcano turns the sky a deep red colour.
That the sun causes the volcano to erupt.
questions.answer: That so much ash fills the sky that the sunlight is hidden.
questions.explanation: The phrase means the eruption throws up so much ash that it blocks the sunlight, darkening the sky for miles around.

questions.prompt: In "These plates drift very slowly" (lines 6–7), what type of word is "slowly"?
questions.explanation: ‘Slowly’ tells us how the plates drift, describing the verb ‘drift’, so it is an adverb.

the giant slabs of rock that make up the Earth’s surface
made liquid by very great heat
melted rock while it is still underground
melted rock once it has burst out onto the surface

activities.title: The Journey of a Raindrop
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage following a single raindrop through the water cycle, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Journey of a Raindrop
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Far out at sea, beneath a blazing summer
sun, a single droplet of water is about to
begin an astonishing journey. The warmth
of the sunlight heats the surface of the
ocean until the droplet, along with countless
others, slowly turns into an invisible gas
called water vapour. This process is known as
evaporation, and it lifts the water gently
upwards into the sky, leaving the salt of the
sea behind in the waves below.
As the vapour rises, the air around it grows
steadily colder. High above the ground, the
tiny droplet cools and gathers with millions
of its companions, clinging to specks of dust
floating in the atmosphere. Together they form
the soft, billowing clouds we see drifting
across the sky. Scientists call this clever
transformation condensation, the moment when
a gas turns patiently back into liquid water.
For days the droplet may travel inside its
cloud, carried by the wind across hills, towns
and distant mountains. Eventually the cloud
becomes so heavy and crowded that it can hold
no more. The droplets join together, growing
larger and heavier, until gravity finally tugs
them downwards. They tumble to the earth as
precipitation, falling as rain, or as delicate
snowflakes when the air is bitterly cold.
Our droplet lands high on a windswept
mountainside as a glittering bead of rain. It
trickles between rocks, joining a bubbling
stream that hurries downhill. The stream meets
others and swells into a broad, winding river,
tumbling over waterfalls and sliding through
quiet valleys. Along the way the water gives a
drink to thirsty plants, animals and people,
before flowing onwards, always seeking the
lowest ground it can possibly find.
At last, after many miles, the river reaches
the coast and pours back into the vast,
shimmering ocean where the journey first began.
There the patient droplet waits once more,
ready for the sun to warm it and lift it skyward
again. This endless loop of evaporation,
condensation and precipitation is called the
water cycle, and it has been repeating itself
for billions of years. Every drop of water you
drink today is part of this ancient, ceaseless
journey around our remarkable planet.

As the vapour rises, the air around it grows steadily colder. High above the ground, the tiny droplet cools and gathers with millions of its companions, clinging to specks of dust floating in the atmosphere. Together they form the soft, billowing clouds we see drifting across the sky. Scientists call this clever transformation condensation, the moment when a gas turns patiently back into liquid water.

For days the droplet may travel inside its cloud, carried by the wind across hills, towns and distant mountains. Eventually the cloud becomes so heavy and crowded that it can hold no more. The droplets join together, growing larger and heavier, until gravity finally tugs them downwards. They tumble to the earth as precipitation, falling as rain, or as delicate snowflakes when the air is bitterly cold.

Our droplet lands high on a windswept mountainside as a glittering bead of rain. It trickles between rocks, joining a bubbling stream that hurries downhill. The stream meets others and swells into a broad, winding river, tumbling over waterfalls and sliding through quiet valleys. Along the way the water gives a drink to thirsty plants, animals and people, before flowing onwards, always seeking the lowest ground it can possibly find.

At last, after many miles, the river reaches the coast and pours back into the vast, shimmering ocean where the journey first began. There the patient droplet waits once more, ready for the sun to warm it and lift it skyward again. This endless loop of evaporation, condensation and precipitation is called the water cycle, and it has been repeating itself for billions of years. Every drop of water you drink today is part of this ancient, ceaseless journey around our remarkable planet.

questions.prompt: What causes the droplet to turn into water vapour at the start?
options: The cold air high above the ground.
The warmth of the sunlight heating the ocean.
The wind blowing across the waves.
The weight of the heavy clouds.

activities.title: Lights in the Deep
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about bioluminescence in the deep ocean, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: Lights in the Deep
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Far below the sunlit surface of the ocean
lies a world of almost total darkness. Sunlight
cannot reach these crushing depths, yet the
water is not entirely black. Here and there,
tiny sparks and ghostly glows drift through the
gloom. This living light is called bioluminescence,
and it is one of the strangest wonders of our
planet. Astonishingly, scientists believe that most
creatures in the deep sea can produce light of
their own.
The glow is created by a clever chemical reaction.
Inside the animal, a substance called luciferin
reacts with oxygen, helped along by a special
chemical known as an enzyme. As the two combine,
energy is released, not as heat but as light. For
this reason it is often described as “cold light”,
because almost none of the energy is wasted as
warmth. Some animals make these chemicals
themselves, while others borrow glowing bacteria
and keep them safely inside their bodies.
Why would a creature want to shine in the dark?
The reasons are surprisingly varied. Some animals
glow to lure their prey closer, dangling a bright
speck like bait until a curious fish swims near. The
famous anglerfish does exactly this, using a glowing
lure that hangs above its enormous, toothy mouth.
Other creatures flash their lights to attract a mate,
signalling across the darkness to others of their
kind.
Light can also be a weapon or a shield. When a
predator attacks, certain jellyfish erupt into
dazzling, spinning patterns that startle and confuse
the hunter, giving the jellyfish a chance to escape.
Cleverer still, some small animals glow gently on
their undersides. Seen from below, their faint light
matches the dim glow of the distant surface, so
they seem to vanish against it. This crafty trick
is known as counter-illumination.
Exploring this hidden world is far from easy. The
deep sea is colder, darker and more crushing than
almost anywhere on Earth, and no human could
survive there unprotected. Instead, scientists send
down sturdy submarines and robotic vehicles fitted
with cameras and powerful lights. Switching their
own lamps off, researchers wait patiently in the
blackness, watching the deep flicker and shimmer
with its own mysterious glow. Every voyage reveals
new species, reminding us how much of our ocean
remains unexplored.

The famous anglerfish does exactly this, using a glowing lure that hangs above its enormous, toothy mouth. Other creatures flash their lights to attract a mate, signalling across the darkness to others of their kind.

Light can also be a weapon or a shield. When a predator attacks, certain jellyfish erupt into dazzling, spinning patterns that startle and confuse the hunter, giving the jellyfish a chance to escape. Cleverer still, some small animals glow gently on their undersides. Seen from below, their faint light matches the dim glow of the distant surface, so they seem to vanish against it. This crafty trick is known as counter-illumination.

Exploring this hidden world is far from easy. The deep sea is colder, darker and more crushing than almost anywhere on Earth, and no human could survive there unprotected. Instead, scientists send down sturdy submarines and robotic vehicles fitted with cameras and powerful lights. Every voyage reveals new species, reminding us how much of our ocean remains unexplored.

activities.title: The Tireless Human Heart
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about the human heart, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Tireless Human Heart
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Inside your chest, working quietly day and
night, lies one of the most remarkable organs
in the human body: the heart. It sits between
the two lungs, slightly to the left of the
centre, and is roughly the size of a clenched
fist. Although it never rests, most people
barely notice it beating away beneath their
ribs, faithfully keeping them alive every
single second of their lives.
The heart is, in simple terms, a muscular
pump. Its task is to push blood around the
body so that every part receives the oxygen
and nutrients it needs. With each beat, the
heart squeezes tightly and then relaxes,
driving blood through a vast network of
tubes. If you stretched out all these tubes
and laid them end to end, they would reach
an astonishing distance, far longer than you
might ever imagine.
The heart itself is divided into four hollow
spaces called chambers. The two upper
chambers receive blood as it returns to the
heart, while the two lower chambers, which
are stronger, pump it out again. Small flaps
called valves sit between the chambers and
act like one-way doors, making sure the blood
always flows in the correct direction and
never slips backwards.
Blood travels through two different kinds of
vessel. Arteries are thick, springy tubes that
carry blood away from the heart, often at
considerable pressure. Veins, on the other
hand, are thinner and carry blood back
towards the heart once the oxygen has been
delivered. Together, these vessels form a loop
that the blood travels around again and again,
all through your life.
You may have noticed your heart pounding
after you sprint or climb the stairs. During
exercise, your muscles demand extra oxygen,
so the heart beats faster to deliver it more
quickly. This is perfectly normal and even
helpful, because regular exercise gradually
strengthens the heart, just as lifting weights
strengthens the arms.
Looking after this faithful organ is easier
than you might think. Eating plenty of fruit
and vegetables, exercising often, sleeping
well and avoiding cigarettes all help to keep
the heart healthy for many years. Treat it
kindly, and this tireless little pump will
carry on beating, beat after beat, for a
wonderfully long lifetime.

Veins, on the other hand, are thinner and carry blood back towards the heart once the oxygen has been delivered. Together, these vessels form a loop that the blood travels around again and again, all through your life.

You may have noticed your heart pounding after you sprint or climb the stairs. During exercise, your muscles demand extra oxygen, so the heart beats faster to deliver it more quickly. This is perfectly normal and even helpful, because regular exercise gradually strengthens the heart, just as lifting weights strengthens the arms.

Looking after this faithful organ is easier than you might think. Eating plenty of fruit and vegetables, exercising often, sleeping well and avoiding cigarettes all help to keep the heart healthy for many years. Treat it kindly, and this tireless little pump will carry on beating, beat after beat, for a wonderfully long lifetime.

activities.title: The Great Migration
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about bird migration, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Great Migration
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Each year, billions of birds undertake one of
nature’s most astonishing journeys. As the seasons
turn, they abandon their homes and travel vast
distances across the planet, a yearly movement
that scientists call migration. Some birds fly only
a short way, but others cross entire oceans and
continents, braving storms, hunger and exhaustion
to reach the very same destination every single year.
But why do birds put themselves through such an
ordeal? The answer lies chiefly in food and
breeding. When winter grips the northern lands,
insects vanish and seeds become scarce, so many
birds flee southward to warmer regions where meals
are plentiful. In spring they return north again,
for the long summer days there are perfect for
raising hungry chicks, with fewer predators about
and an abundance of food close at hand.
The distances some birds cover are almost beyond
belief. The undisputed champion is the Arctic tern,
a small seabird that flies from the Arctic to the
Antarctic and back each year. Over a single
lifetime, this tireless traveller may cover more
than two million kilometres — roughly the same
as flying to the Moon and back three times.
Few creatures on Earth journey so far.
How birds find their way is one of the great
mysteries of the natural world. Many navigate by
the position of the sun during the day and by the
patterns of the stars at night. Most remarkable
of all, some birds can sense the Earth’s magnetic
field, using it like an invisible compass to keep
themselves pointed in the right direction. Familiar
landmarks, such as coastlines and rivers, help to
guide them safely along their ancient routes.
Yet migration is fraught with peril. Fierce storms
can blow exhausted flocks far out to sea, while
hawks and falcons hunt the weary travellers from
above. Many birds perish simply because they cannot
find enough food to fuel such a demanding flight.
In recent times, tall buildings, bright city lights
and shrinking wild habitats have made these journeys
more dangerous than ever before for migrating birds.
Despite every hazard, the great migration continues,
just as it has for countless thousands of years.
It is a powerful reminder of how cleverly animals
are suited to their world, and of how much there
still remains for us to discover and to protect.

activities.title: The Secret of Spider Silk
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A non-fiction passage about spider silk, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Secret of Spider Silk
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Most people hurry past a spider’s web without a
second glance, yet they are walking past one of
nature’s most astonishing materials. Spider silk is
produced inside the spider’s body as a liquid, then
drawn out through tiny nozzles called spinnerets,
usually found near the tip of the abdomen. As the
liquid is pulled into the air, it hardens almost
instantly into a thread that is finer than a human
hair yet remarkably difficult to break.
Although the thread looks delicate, it is one of the
strongest natural materials we know. Weight for
weight, spider silk is stronger than steel, and it
can stretch far without snapping. If a strand of
silk were as thick as a pencil, scientists believe
it could stop an aeroplane in flight. This unusual
blend of strength and stretchiness is what makes
the material so extraordinary, and it explains why
researchers have studied it for many years.
A single spider does not make just one kind of
silk. Most spiders can produce several different
types, each suited to a particular task. Sticky,
elastic silk forms the spiral of a web, ready to
trap any insect that blunders into it. A stronger,
non-sticky silk creates the spokes and the outer
frame that hold the whole web together. The spider
itself walks only on these dry threads, so it does
not become stuck in its own clever trap.
Silk is useful even when there is no web at all.
As a spider moves about, it often trails a single
thread behind it, called a dragline. This acts like
a safety rope: if the spider is knocked from a leaf,
it can dangle in mid-air and then climb back to
safety. Many spiders also use silk to wrap up their
captured prey, bundling it tightly so that it cannot
wriggle free, and some spin soft silken cases to
protect their precious eggs.
Because spider silk is so light, strong and flexible,
scientists are eager to copy it. They hope one day
to weave artificial silk into bandages that help
wounds heal, into ropes and nets that are tough but
feather-light, and even into protective clothing.
Farming spiders is impractical, however, for the
creatures are fiercely territorial and would simply
eat one another. Instead, researchers are learning
to recreate the silk in laboratories, hoping to
borrow one of nature’s oldest and best-kept
secrets.

This unusual blend of strength and stretchiness is what makes the material so extraordinary, and it explains why researchers have studied it for many years.

A single spider does not make just one kind of silk. Most spiders can produce several different types, each suited to a particular task. Sticky, elastic silk forms the spiral of a web, ready to trap any insect that blunders into it. A stronger, non-sticky silk creates the spokes and the outer frame that hold the whole web together. The spider itself walks only on these dry threads, so it does not become stuck in its own clever trap.

Silk is useful even when there is no web at all. As a spider moves about, it often trails a single thread behind it, called a dragline. This acts like a safety rope: if the spider is knocked from a leaf, it can dangle in mid-air and then climb back to safety. Many spiders also use silk to wrap up their captured prey, bundling it tightly so that it cannot wriggle free, and some spin soft silken cases to protect their precious eggs.

Because spider silk is so light, strong and flexible, scientists are eager to copy it. They hope one day to weave artificial silk into bandages that help wounds heal, into ropes and nets that are tough but feather-light, and even into protective clothing. Farming spiders is impractical, however, for the creatures are fiercely territorial and would simply eat one another. Instead, researchers are learning to recreate the silk in laboratories, hoping to borrow one of nature’s oldest and best-kept secrets.

passage.title: The Living Rainforest
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Tropical rainforests are among the oldest and most
remarkable habitats on our planet. They grow in a
warm, damp band close to the equator, where rain
falls almost every single day of the year. Although
they cover only a small fraction of the Earth’s land,
these forests are home to more than half of all the
plant and animal species that scientists have ever
discovered. Step inside one, and you enter a green,
dripping world that is humming with hidden life.
A rainforest is built in four distinct layers, each
with its own special community of living things. On
the shadowy forest floor, very little sunlight breaks
through, so fallen leaves rot quickly in the gloom.
Above this lies the understorey, a tangle of shrubs
and young trees where insects and frogs thrive. Higher
still spreads the canopy, a vast leafy roof crowded
with monkeys, birds and countless climbing creatures.
Finally, a few giant trees push through as the
emergent layer, towering above everything below.
The sheer variety of life in a rainforest is almost
impossible to imagine. A single tree may shelter
hundreds of different beetles, while brilliant orchids
and trailing vines cling to its branches. Jaguars,
sloths, parrots and tree frogs all make their homes
here, alongside millions of insects that have never
even been named. Many of the medicines we depend on
today were first discovered among these abundant
and astonishing plants.
Rainforests matter far beyond their own borders. The
trees breathe in carbon dioxide and release the oxygen
that living things need, which is why rainforests are
sometimes called the lungs of the planet. They also
soak up rainfall and release moisture into the air,
helping to shape weather and climate in distant lands.
Without these forests, our whole world would be a
hotter and far less stable place to live.
Yet rainforests are vanishing at an alarming rate.
Vast areas are cut down for timber, or burned to clear
land for farming and cattle, a destruction known as
deforestation. When the trees disappear, the creatures
that depend on them lose their homes, and the soil
that remains is quickly washed away by the rain.
Each year, an area of forest larger than many whole
countries is lost forever.
Thankfully, rainforests can still be protected. Some
are now guarded as national parks, where logging is
forbidden and rare animals are kept safe. Shoppers
can choose timber and food grown in sustainable ways,
and charities are helping local people to replant
trees that were felled long ago. By making careful
choices, every one of us can help these magnificent
forests survive for generations still to come.

They grow in a warm, damp band close to the equator, where rain falls almost every single day of the year. Step inside one, and you enter a green, dripping world that is humming with hidden life.

Above this lies the understorey, a tangle of shrubs and young trees where insects and frogs thrive. Higher still spreads the canopy, a vast leafy roof crowded with monkeys, birds and countless climbing creatures. Finally, a few giant trees push through as the emergent layer, towering above everything below.

The sheer variety of life in a rainforest is almost impossible to imagine. A single tree may shelter hundreds of different beetles, while brilliant orchids and trailing vines cling to its branches. Jaguars, sloths, parrots and tree frogs all make their homes here, alongside millions of insects that have never even been named. Many of the medicines we depend on today were first discovered among these abundant and astonishing plants.

They also soak up rainfall and release moisture into the air, helping to shape weather and climate in distant lands. activities.title: Antarctica: The Frozen Continent

passage.title: Antarctica: The Frozen Continent
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: At the very bottom of our planet lies
Antarctica, a vast continent of ice and snow.
It is the coldest, windiest and driest place
on Earth, yet it is also one of the most
beautiful. In winter the temperature can
plunge below minus sixty degrees, and fierce
winds race across the land at terrifying speed.
No country owns this frozen wilderness; instead,
many nations have agreed to share and protect it.
Almost the whole continent is buried beneath
an enormous ice sheet, which in places is more
than four kilometres thick. This ancient ice
holds most of the fresh water on our planet.
Although snow falls only rarely, the bitter cold
means it almost never melts, so layer upon layer
has built up slowly over millions of years. Because
so little rain or snow falls, Antarctica is
officially classed as a desert, despite the ice.
Few creatures can survive such a harsh climate,
but those that do are wonderfully adapted. Emperor
penguins huddle together in their thousands to share
warmth during the long, dark winter. Seals haul
themselves onto the ice to rest, while enormous
whales glide through the chilly waters offshore,
feasting on tiny shrimp-like creatures called krill.
These animals form a delicate web of life that
depends entirely on the cold seas around the coast.
Antarctica has no permanent human residents, but
scientists travel there to live and work at research
stations dotted across the ice. They endure months
of isolation, studying the weather, the wildlife and
the ancient ice itself. By drilling deep into the
ice sheet, researchers can examine bubbles of air
trapped long ago, which reveal what the atmosphere
was like thousands of years before humans existed.
This makes the frozen continent enormously important
for understanding the Earth’s climate. The ice
preserves a remarkable record of how our planet has
changed, and how it might change in the future. As
the world grows warmer, scientists watch anxiously
to see how quickly the ice is melting, for the
secrets hidden in Antarctica concern every one of us,
wherever in the world we happen to live.
paragraphs: At the very bottom of our planet lies Antarctica, a vast continent of ice and snow. It is the coldest, windiest and driest place on Earth, yet it is also one of the most beautiful. In winter the temperature can plunge below minus sixty degrees, and fierce winds race across the land at terrifying speed. No country owns this frozen wilderness; instead, many nations have agreed to share and protect it.

Almost the whole continent is buried beneath an enormous ice sheet, which in places is more than four kilometres thick. This ancient ice holds most of the fresh water on our planet. Although snow falls only rarely, the bitter cold means it almost never melts, so layer upon layer has built up slowly over millions of years. Because so little rain or snow falls, Antarctica is officially classed as a desert, despite the ice.

Few creatures can survive such a harsh climate, but those that do are wonderfully adapted. Emperor penguins huddle together in their thousands to share warmth during the long, dark winter. Seals haul themselves onto the ice to rest, while enormous whales glide through the chilly waters offshore, feasting on tiny shrimp-like creatures called krill. These animals form a delicate web of life that depends entirely on the cold seas around the coast.

Antarctica has no permanent human residents, but scientists travel there to live and work at research stations dotted across the ice. They endure months of isolation, studying the weather, the wildlife and the ancient ice itself. By drilling deep into the ice sheet, researchers can examine bubbles of air trapped long ago, which reveal what the atmosphere was like thousands of years before humans existed.

This makes the frozen continent enormously important for understanding the Earth’s climate. The ice preserves a remarkable record of how our planet has changed, and how it might change in the future. As the world grows warmer, scientists watch anxiously to see how quickly the ice is melting, for the secrets hidden in Antarctica concern every one of us, wherever in the world we happen to live.

activities.title: The Plastic Problem

activities.description: A non-fiction passage about ocean plastic pollution, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: The Plastic Problem
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Plastic is one of the most useful materials
ever invented. It is cheap to make, light
to carry and astonishingly strong. We use
it for bottles, toys, packaging, pipes and
countless other everyday objects. Yet the
very quality that makes plastic so handy is
also what makes it so dangerous: it is built
to last. A plastic bottle dropped today may
still survive, almost unchanged, hundreds of
years from now.
Sadly, a great deal of this plastic ends up
in the sea. Rubbish that is dropped in the
street can be washed into drains, carried
along rivers and finally swept out into the
ocean. Waste from ships and litter left on
beaches add to the problem. Scientists
estimate that millions of tonnes of plastic
pour into the oceans every single year, and
the amount is steadily rising.
Once it reaches the sea, plastic becomes a
deadly trap for wildlife. Turtles often
mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish,
one of their favourite foods, and swallow
them. Seabirds feed brightly coloured scraps
to their hungry chicks, believing the pieces
to be fish. Seals, dolphins and whales can
become tangled in old nets and ropes,
unable to swim or feed. For these creatures,
the consequences are frequently fatal.
Over many years, the pounding waves and
burning sunlight break larger pieces into
tiny fragments known as microplastics. These
specks, smaller than a grain of rice, are
almost impossible to remove from the water.
Worryingly, fish and shellfish eat them by
mistake. Scientists fear that microplastics
may then pass along the food chain, ending
up on the very plates from which we eat our
own dinner.
The good news is that all of us can help to
tackle this problem. We can refuse single-use
plastics, such as straws and flimsy bags, and
choose reusable bottles instead. We can sort
our rubbish carefully so that more of it is
recycled, and we can join beach cleans to
gather litter before it drifts away. Small
actions, repeated by millions of people, can
make an enormous difference to the health of
our precious oceans.
paragraphs: Plastic is one of the most useful materials ever invented. It is cheap to make, light to carry and astonishingly strong. We use it for bottles, toys, packaging, pipes and countless other everyday objects. Yet the very quality that makes plastic so handy is also what makes it so dangerous: it is built to last. A plastic bottle dropped today may still survive, almost unchanged, hundreds of years from now.

Sadly, a great deal of this plastic ends up in the sea. Rubbish that is dropped in the street can be washed into drains, carried along rivers and finally swept out into the ocean. Waste from ships and litter left on beaches add to the problem. Scientists estimate that millions of tonnes of plastic pour into the oceans every single year, and the amount is steadily rising.

Once it reaches the sea, plastic becomes a deadly trap for wildlife. Turtles often mistake floating plastic bags for jellyfish, one of their favourite foods, and swallow them. Seabirds feed brightly coloured scraps to their hungry chicks, believing the pieces to be fish. Seals, dolphins and whales can become tangled in old nets and ropes, unable to swim or feed. For these creatures, the consequences are frequently fatal.

Over many years, the pounding waves and burning sunlight break larger pieces into tiny fragments known as microplastics. These specks, smaller than a grain of rice, are almost impossible to remove from the water. Worryingly, fish and shellfish eat them by mistake. Scientists fear that microplastics may then pass along the food chain, ending up on the very plates from which we eat our own dinner.

The good news is that all of us can help to tackle this problem. We can refuse single-use plastics, such as straws and flimsy bags, and choose reusable bottles instead. We can sort our rubbish carefully so that more of it is recycled, and we can join beach cleans to gather litter before it drifts away. Small actions, repeated by millions of people, can make an enormous difference to the health of our precious oceans.

questions.prompt: According to the text, why is plastic such a useful material?

passage.title: The Great Fire of London
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: In the early hours of Sunday the second of
September 1666, a small fire broke out in a
baker’s shop in Pudding Lane. The baker,
Thomas Farriner, had supposedly forgotten to
put out the embers in his oven the night
before. At first nobody was greatly alarmed,
for small fires were common in the crowded
city. Yet this blaze would soon become one of
the most destructive disasters in the history
of London.
The fire spread with terrifying speed. The
houses of seventeenth-century London were
built mostly of wood and sealed with pitch,
and they leaned so close together that their
upper storeys almost touched across the narrow
streets. A long, dry summer had left the timber
parched, and a strong wind from the east fanned
the flames from one rooftop to the next. Within
hours, whole streets were ablaze, and panic
swept through the sleeping city.
Fighting such a fire was desperately hard. There
were no fire brigades as we know them today,
only leather buckets, hand-pumped squirts and
long hooks. The most effective method was to
pull down or even blow up the houses in the
path of the fire, creating gaps called firebreaks
that the flames could not leap across. Sadly, the
Lord Mayor hesitated to order this, and by the
time it began the fire had already raged out
of control.
Much of what we know comes from Samuel Pepys,
a naval official who kept a famous diary. He
described people fleeing with their belongings,
and frightened pigeons that would not leave
their homes until their wings were singed. Pepys
even buried his precious wine and a wheel of
cheese in his garden to protect them. His vivid
account lets us picture the chaos of those
dreadful days as if we had been there ourselves.
After four long days the wind finally dropped,
and the exhausted citizens managed to halt the
flames. The damage was enormous: more than
thirteen thousand houses and old St Paul’s
Cathedral lay in ruins, and tens of thousands of
people were left homeless. Remarkably, very few
deaths were recorded. London was gradually
rebuilt, this time with wider streets and sturdy
brick and stone instead of timber. The architect
Sir Christopher Wren designed a magnificent new
St Paul’s, and a tall column known as the
Monument still stands today, marking the spot
where the Great Fire began.
paragraphs: In the early hours of Sunday the second of September 1666, a small fire broke out in a baker’s shop in Pudding Lane. The baker, Thomas Farriner, had supposedly forgotten to put out the embers in his oven the night before. At first nobody was greatly alarmed, for small fires were common in the crowded city. Yet this blaze would soon become one of the most destructive disasters in the history of London.

The fire spread with terrifying speed. The houses of seventeenth-century London were built mostly of wood and sealed with pitch, and they leaned so close together that their upper storeys almost touched across the narrow streets. A long, dry summer had left the timber parched, and a strong wind from the east fanned the flames from one rooftop to the next. Within hours, whole streets were ablaze, and panic swept through the sleeping city.

Fighting such a fire was desperately hard. There were no fire brigades as we know them today, only leather buckets, hand-pumped squirts and long hooks. The most effective method was to pull down or even blow up the houses in the path of the fire, creating gaps called firebreaks that the flames could not leap across. Sadly, the Lord Mayor hesitated to order this, and by the time it began the fire had already raged out of control.

Much of what we know comes from Samuel Pepys, a naval official who kept a famous diary. He described people fleeing with their belongings, and frightened pigeons that would not leave their homes until their wings were singed. Pepys even buried his precious wine and a wheel of cheese in his garden to protect them. His vivid account lets us picture the chaos of those dreadful days as if we had been there ourselves.

After four long days the wind finally dropped, and the exhausted citizens managed to halt the flames. The damage was enormous: more than thirteen thousand houses and old St Paul’s Cathedral lay in ruins, and tens of thousands of people were left homeless. Remarkably, very few deaths were recorded. London was gradually rebuilt, this time with wider streets and sturdy brick and stone instead of timber. The architect Sir Christopher Wren designed a magnificent new St Paul’s, and a tall column known as the Monument still stands today, marking the spot where the Great Fire began.

activities.title: Secrets of the Pyramids
activities.subject: English

passage.title: Secrets of the Pyramids
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Few buildings on Earth have astonished people for as
long as the pyramids of ancient Egypt. Rising from
the desert sands more than four thousand years ago,
these enormous stone monuments were not palaces or
temples but tombs. The ancient Egyptians believed
that a pharaoh, their god-king, would continue to
live on after death, so they built him a magnificent
resting place to protect his body for eternity.
The most famous of all is the Great Pyramid of
Giza, constructed for the pharaoh Khufu. When it
was finished, it stood roughly one hundred and forty
metres tall, and for almost four thousand years it
remained the tallest building made by human hands.
It was assembled from more than two million blocks
of stone, and some of these blocks weighed as much
as several family cars. The sheer scale of the
structure still leaves visitors speechless today.
How the Egyptians moved such colossal blocks without
modern machines remains a puzzle. For a long time
people imagined that slaves were forced to drag the
stones, but most experts now disagree. The pyramids
were probably raised by thousands of skilled workers
who were paid in bread, beer and grain. These
labourers may have hauled the blocks up long ramps
of mud and rubble, sliding them on wooden sledges.
Pouring water onto the sand in front of a sledge
made it glide far more easily, lessening the effort
needed to shift each heavy load into position.
Deep inside the pyramid lay the burial chamber,
where the pharaoh’s body was carefully preserved as
a mummy. Around him the Egyptians placed dazzling
treasures: golden masks, fine jewellery, furniture,
weapons and jars of food. They believed the dead
king would need these possessions in the afterlife.
Hidden passages, false doors and heavy stone slabs
were designed to baffle robbers, yet over the
centuries thieves still plundered most of the tombs.
Even now, the pyramids guard their secrets jealously.
Archaeologists continue to argue about exactly how
the ramps were built and how the workers were fed.
In recent years, scanning equipment has revealed
mysterious hidden spaces within the Great Pyramid
that nobody has yet entered or explained. Each fresh
discovery reminds us that these ancient wonders,
though silent and weathered, have not finished
telling their remarkable story.
paragraphs: Few buildings on Earth have astonished people for as long as the pyramids of ancient Egypt. Rising from the desert sands more than four thousand years ago, these enormous stone monuments were not palaces or temples but tombs. The ancient Egyptians believed that a pharaoh, their god-king, would continue to live on after death, so they built him a magnificent resting place to protect his body for eternity.

The most famous of all is the Great Pyramid of Giza, constructed for the pharaoh Khufu. It was assembled from more than two million blocks of stone, and some of these blocks weighed as much as several family cars. The sheer scale of the structure still leaves visitors speechless today.

How the Egyptians moved such colossal blocks without modern machines remains a puzzle. For a long time people imagined that slaves were forced to drag the stones, but most experts now disagree. The pyramids were probably raised by thousands of skilled workers who were paid in bread, beer and grain. These labourers may have hauled the blocks up long ramps of mud and rubble, sliding them on wooden sledges. Pouring water onto the sand in front of a sledge made it glide far more easily, lessening the effort needed to shift each heavy load into position.

Deep inside the pyramid lay the burial chamber, where the pharaoh’s body was carefully preserved as a mummy. Around him the Egyptians placed dazzling treasures: golden masks, fine jewellery, furniture, weapons and jars of food. They believed the dead king would need these possessions in the afterlife. Hidden passages, false doors and heavy stone slabs were designed to baffle robbers, yet over the centuries thieves still plundered most of the tombs.

Even now, the pyramids guard their secrets jealously. Archaeologists continue to argue about exactly how the ramps were built and how the workers were fed. In recent years, scanning equipment has revealed mysterious hidden spaces within the Great Pyramid that nobody has yet entered or explained. Each fresh discovery reminds us that these ancient wonders, though silent and weathered, have not finished telling their remarkable story.

activities.title: The Age of Steam
activities.subject: English

passage.title: The Age of Steam
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: When Queen Victoria came to the throne in
1837, most people in Britain travelled no
faster than a galloping horse. Roads were
rough and journeys were slow, jolting and
tiring. Within a single lifetime, however, the
steam railway transformed the country beyond
all recognition, knitting distant towns and
cities together as never before.
At the heart of this revolution stood the steam
locomotive. Inside its great iron boiler, a
roaring coal fire heated water until it became
steam. As the steam expanded, it pushed
pistons back and forth, and these pistons
turned the heavy driving wheels. Hissing and
clanking, belching smoke and sparks, these
powerful machines could haul enormous loads
at speeds that astonished everyone who saw
them thundering past.
Building the railways was gruelling work. An
army of labourers known as navvies dug the
lines by hand, armed with little more than
picks, shovels and barrows. They sliced deep
cuttings through hills, raised towering
embankments across valleys, and bored long,
dark tunnels through solid rock. The work was
dangerous and exhausting, yet the navvies laid
thousands of miles of track in only a few
remarkable decades.
The effects of the railways spread far and
wide. Fresh food, coal and post could now be
carried swiftly between towns, so trade
flourished and shops were better stocked than
ever. Ordinary families, who had rarely left
their own villages, could suddenly afford a
cheap ticket to the seaside. Bustling resorts
such as Blackpool and Margate grew quickly,
with piers, donkey rides and ice cream for
delighted day-trippers.
The railways even changed the clocks. Before
trains, each town kept its own local time,
guided by the sun, so noon in Bristol differed
from noon in London. Such muddles made
timetables nearly impossible. To solve this,
the railway companies adopted a single
standard time across the whole country. By the
end of Victoria's reign, the age of steam had
reshaped not only how people travelled, but how
they worked, shopped and measured the very day.

passage.title: Life in Roman Britain
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: When the Romans invaded Britain almost two
thousand years ago, they brought with them a way
of living that was very different from anything
the island had seen before. Over the years that
followed, they transformed a land of scattered
villages into a province dotted with busy towns,
grand buildings and a network of remarkable roads.
Much of what they built can still be traced today.
The Romans were skilled and ambitious builders.
They founded towns such as London, York and Bath,
laying them out in neat grids of straight streets.
At the heart of each town stood a forum, an open
marketplace where people gathered to trade goods,
hear news and settle arguments. Their roads were
famously straight, driving directly across hills and
marshes so that soldiers and traders could journey
swiftly from one corner of the country to another.
Many modern roads still follow these ancient routes.
Life in a Roman town could be surprisingly
comfortable. Public baths were among the most
popular places of all, where people went not only
to wash but also to exercise, relax and chatter with
friends. The wealthiest families lived in spacious
villas in the countryside. Beneath the floors of these
villas ran a clever heating system called a hypocaust,
which allowed warm air from a furnace to circulate
and keep the rooms cosy even in the coldest weather.
Ordinary people lived more simply, yet they still
enjoyed new comforts. They ate bread, vegetables
and porridge, flavoured with herbs and tangy
sauces the Romans adored. Wealthier diners feasted
on meat, fish and sweet honeyed cakes. Most people
wore a simple woollen tunic, while richer citizens
draped themselves in a toga, a long garment that
showed off their status. Markets brimmed with
pottery, jewellery and goods carried from distant
lands across the wide Roman empire.
After about four hundred years, the Romans finally
departed, called home to defend their crumbling
empire. Without them, the grand towns slowly fell
into ruin, and many of their clever inventions were
forgotten for centuries. Yet they had left a lasting
mark upon the land. Their roads, the foundations of
their buildings and even some of their words still
survive, quietly reminding us of the time when
Britain was a thriving part of the Roman world.

Many modern roads still follow these ancient routes.

passage.title: Marie Curie: A Life in Science
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Marie Curie was one of the greatest
scientists who has ever lived. She was born
in 1867 in Warsaw, the capital of Poland,
and was given the name Maria Skłodowska.
As a young girl, she loved learning and read
every book she could find. Her father, who
was a teacher, encouraged her curiosity, and
she quickly proved herself a brilliant pupil.
At that time, however, the universities in
Poland did not allow women to study. Maria
refused to give up her dream of an education.
She worked for several years as a governess,
teaching other families’ children, and saved
what little money she earned. At last, in 1891,
she travelled to Paris to study at the famous
Sorbonne university, where women were rare
in the science classrooms. There she changed
her name to the French form, Marie.
Life in Paris was hard. Marie had very little
money and often went hungry, living in a cold
attic room and studying late into the night.
Yet she was determined, and she earned top
marks in both physics and mathematics. It was
in Paris that she met another young scientist,
Pierre Curie. The pair shared a deep love of
research, and in 1895 they married, becoming
partners in life and in the laboratory.
Together, Marie and Pierre worked tirelessly,
investigating strange rays that came from
certain rocks. Marie called this mysterious
energy “radioactivity”. Stirring great vats of
ore by hand in a draughty shed, the Curies
discovered two new elements, which Marie
named polonium, after her homeland, and
radium. Their work was exhausting, but it
opened a doorway into an unknown world.
In 1903 Marie shared the Nobel Prize for
Physics with Pierre and another scientist. She
was the first woman ever to receive one. After
Pierre died in a tragic accident, Marie carried
on alone, and in 1911 she won a second Nobel
Prize, this time for Chemistry. No one before
her had won the prize twice, in two different
fields of science.
Marie Curie died in 1934, worn out by years
of working close to dangerous radiation whose
perils were not yet understood. Even so, her
discoveries transformed medicine, helping
doctors to treat illnesses such as cancer. Today
she is remembered not only as a remarkable
scientist but as an inspiration, proving that
determination can overcome almost any obstacle.

activities.title: Amelia Earhart: Queen of the Skies

passage.title: Amelia Earhart: Queen of the Skies
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Amelia Earhart was born in Kansas in 1897,
long before flying was an everyday sight. As a
young girl she was bold and curious, climbing
trees and hunting rats with a rifle, refusing to
behave the way girls were expected to behave.
Few people then could have guessed that this
restless child would one day soar into history.
Amelia’s love of flying began at an air show,
where a pilot swooped low above the watching
crowd. Most people darted away in fright, but
Amelia stood perfectly still, thrilled rather than
frightened. Years later she took her very first
flight as a passenger. The moment the aircraft
lifted from the ground, she knew that she simply
had to learn to fly an aeroplane herself.
In those days, female pilots were extremely rare,
and many believed that flying was no place for a
woman. Amelia ignored their doubts completely.
She worked long hours at dull jobs to pay for her
lessons, cut her hair short like other aviators,
and slept beside her new leather flying jacket so
that it would look worn and well used. In 1921
she bought a small bright-yellow plane, which she
fondly nicknamed the Canary.
Amelia’s greatest triumph came in May 1932,
when she became the first woman to fly alone
across the Atlantic Ocean. The journey was
terrifying: thick fog hid the stars, ice formed on
the wings, and a fuel leak threatened disaster.
Through it all she remained calm and determined,
guiding her aircraft safely to a quiet field in
Ireland. Overnight she became famous around the
world, admired for her courage and her remarkable
skill.
Amelia never stopped chasing new challenges. In
1937 she set out to fly all the way around the
globe, following a route close to the equator. She
had completed most of the enormous journey when,
somewhere over the vast Pacific Ocean, her plane
vanished without a trace. Despite an immense
search, neither Amelia nor her aircraft was ever
found, and her sudden disappearance remains one
of history’s most enduring mysteries.
Today Amelia Earhart is remembered as far more
than a record breaker. She showed the whole world
that bravery and ambition belong to everyone, and
that no goal is too distant to pursue. Her name
still inspires young people to dream boldly and to
reach, quite literally, for the skies.

activities.title: Mary Anning and the Fossil Hunters
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A biographical non-fiction passage about the fossil hunter Mary Anning, testing retrieval, inference, vocabulary and grammar.

passage.title: Mary Anning and the Fossil Hunters
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: More than two hundred years ago, a young girl
named Mary Anning spent her days searching the
windswept cliffs of Lyme Regis, on the south coast
of England. Born in 1799 into a poor family, Mary
learned to hunt for fossils from her father, who
sold the curious stone shells and strange remains
to visitors as seaside souvenirs. When he died,
the family was left with very little money, and
Mary continued the dangerous work to help them.
The cliffs at Lyme Regis were treacherous places.
After heavy winter storms, great chunks of rock
would crumble and slide down towards the beach.
These landslides exposed fresh fossils that had
been buried for millions of years, yet they could
also bury a careless hunter in an instant. Mary
learned to work quickly, prising her discoveries
from the rock before the next collapse swept them
away. On one occasion a slide killed her faithful
dog, Tray, who had followed her along the shore.
When she was only twelve, Mary made an astonishing
find. Together with her brother she uncovered the
first complete skeleton of an ichthyosaur, a huge
dolphin-shaped reptile that had swum in ancient
seas. At the time, nobody fully understood what
such a creature was, and the discovery amazed the
scientists who came to study it. Years later, Mary
made another remarkable breakthrough: she dug out
the first skeleton of a long-necked sea reptile
called a plesiosaur, an animal so peculiar that
some experts at first refused to believe it real.
Despite her brilliance, Mary faced many obstacles.
She was poor, she was a woman, and she had little
formal schooling, yet she taught herself geology
by reading borrowed books and copying out their
diagrams by hand. In those days, women were not
allowed to join scientific societies or to publish
their findings. Wealthy gentlemen would buy Mary’s
fossils, write about them in important journals,
and take the credit for themselves, while her own
name was quietly left out of the story.
Mary Anning died in 1847, never receiving the full
recognition she deserved during her lifetime. Today,
however, she is celebrated as one of the most
important fossil hunters who ever lived. Her
patient, painstaking work helped to prove that the
world was far older than people had imagined, and
that strange creatures had once ruled the seas. The
shy girl from Lyme Regis, who chipped away at the
cliffs in all weathers, changed our understanding
of the history of life on Earth.

activities.title: The Lighthouse Keeper

passage.title: The Lighthouse Keeper
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: The storm arrived just after dusk, hurling itself
against the lighthouse like a furious giant. Rain
lashed the windows, and the wind screamed through
every crack in the old stone walls. Mara pressed
her nose to the glass and watched the black sea
rise and fall, its white crests glowing faintly in
the dark. She had never seen the ocean so angry.
“Grandad,” she called, her voice small, “is the
lamp still burning?” Old Tom Pellew did not look
up from the brass machinery he was tending. His
weathered hands moved with the calm of a man who
had kept this light for forty winters. “It must
burn, child,” he murmured, “or the boats out there
will never find their way home tonight.”
Mara knew the words by heart, yet tonight they
frightened her. Somewhere beyond the churning
water was the Stella Maris, the little fishing boat
that her uncle sailed. It should have returned
hours ago. As if reading her thoughts, Grandad
rested a heavy hand on her shoulder. “Trust the
light, Mara. It has guided sailors home through
worse nights than this one. Now, fetch the oil.”
She hurried down the winding stairs, her candle
flickering, and heaved the oil can back up step
by step. Her arms ached and her heart hammered,
but she would not let the great lamp falter. When
the flame dipped low, they fed it together, and
the beam swept out once more across the heaving
waves, steady and bright and unbroken.
Then Mara saw it — a tiny smudge of red bobbing
far out among the breakers. “There!” she gasped,
pointing into the gloom. Grandad seized his old
telescope and pressed it to his eye. For a long
moment he said nothing at all. Then a slow,
weathered smile spread across his face. “That’s
the Stella Maris,” he breathed. “She’s turning
towards us. She has seen our light.”
Together they watched the brave little boat crawl
homeward, climbing each mountain of water and
sliding safely down the other side. By the time
it slipped at last into the sheltered harbour, the
first grey light of dawn was creeping over the
cliffs. Mara, exhausted and proud, leaned against
her grandfather. The storm had not beaten them,
and the faithful old lamp had done its work.

options: He is building suspense as he makes sure of what he sees.
He cannot see anything at all in the gloom.
He is angry that Mara has distracted him.
He has decided the boat is lost for good.
He is too tired to keep watching the sea.
questions.answer: He is building suspense as he makes sure of what he sees.
questions.explanation: He says nothing 'for a long moment' and then smiles, showing he was carefully checking before sharing the good news.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements is TRUE?
options: Mara let the great lamp go out during the storm.
The Stella Maris sank before reaching the harbour.
Grandad refused to let Mara help with the light.
The boat reached the harbour safely by dawn.
Mara never managed to carry the oil up the stairs.
questions.answer: The boat reached the harbour safely by dawn.
questions.explanation: The boat 'slipped at last into the sheltered harbour' as 'the first grey light of dawn' arrived. The other options all contradict the story.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "weathered" (line 12)?
questions.explanation: 'Weathered' describes hands that are worn and aged by years of hard work in wind and sea.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "falter" (line 28)?
questions.explanation: To 'falter' is to weaken or fail; Mara refuses to let the lamp grow weak and go out.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "hurling itself against the lighthouse like a furious giant" (lines 1–2) suggest about the storm?
options: The storm is gentle and slow to arrive.
The storm is far away and harmless.
The storm is violent and powerful, attacking the lighthouse.
The storm is shaped exactly like a giant.
The storm has already begun to fade away.
questions.answer: The storm is violent and powerful, attacking the lighthouse.
questions.explanation: Comparing the storm to a furious giant throwing itself at the tower shows how violent and powerful it is.

questions.prompt: In "the black sea rise and fall" (lines 5–6), what type of word is "black"?
questions.explanation: 'Black' describes the noun 'sea', telling us its colour, so it is an adjective.
