activities.title: The Locked Garden
activities.subject: English
activities.description: A fiction story extract about a curious child who finds a way into a long-forgotten walled garden.

passage.title: The Locked Garden
passage.subtitle: Read this passage carefully and answer the questions that follow.
lines: Maya had been at Great-Aunt Edith’s house
for three rainy days before she found the wall.
The house was an old, rambling place, full of
creaking floors and rooms that smelled of dust
and lavender. Edith spent her afternoons dozing
in a high-backed chair, so Maya was left to
wander the long corridors entirely alone.
On the fourth morning the rain finally stopped.
Maya slipped outside and followed a mossy path
to the very back of the tangled garden. There,
half hidden behind a curtain of ivy, stood a wall
far taller than she was. Set into it was a wooden
door, its green paint peeling, and across it hung
a heavy iron chain, locked tight and red with rust.
She tugged at the chain, but it would not move.
“What is in there?” she whispered to herself.
Curiosity tugged at her like a fish on a line.
Maya walked the length of the wall, trailing her
fingers along the cold brick, searching for a clue.
At last, where the ivy grew thickest, she felt the
bricks give way to a narrow gap, just wide enough
for a determined girl to wriggle through. Her
heart hammering, she pushed into the leaves and
squeezed into the darkness beyond.
For a moment she could see nothing at all. Then
her eyes adjusted, and her breath caught in her
throat. The garden had not died, as she expected;
it had run wild and gloriously free. Roses climbed
in great untrimmed arches overhead, and the air
was thick with the hum of a thousand drowsy bees.
A fountain, long silent, brimmed with rainwater,
and around it grew flowers in every colour she
could name, and several she could not.
In the centre stood a small stone statue of a
girl, her arms outstretched as if to welcome
visitors after all these years. Beneath it, almost
swallowed by grass, lay a flat carved plaque.
Maya knelt and brushed the moss aside with
trembling hands. “For Edith,” it read, “who loved
this garden best of all.” Maya sat back on her
heels, suddenly understanding why the door had
been chained, and why nobody ever spoke of it.
She would tell her great-aunt what she had found,
she decided. Perhaps, between the two of them,
the garden need not stay forgotten any longer.
paragraphs: Maya had been at Great-Aunt Edith’s house for three rainy days before she found the wall. The house was an old, rambling place, full of creaking floors and rooms that smelled of dust and lavender. Edith spent her afternoons dozing in a high-backed chair, so Maya was left to wander the long corridors entirely alone.

On the fourth morning the rain finally stopped. Maya slipped outside and followed a mossy path to the very back of the tangled garden. There, half hidden behind a curtain of ivy, stood a wall far taller than she was. Set into it was a wooden door, its green paint peeling, and across it hung a heavy iron chain, locked tight and red with rust. She tugged at the chain, but it would not move.

“What is in there?” she whispered to herself. Curiosity tugged at her like a fish on a line. Maya walked the length of the wall, trailing her fingers along the cold brick, searching for a clue. At last, where the ivy grew thickest, she felt the bricks give way to a narrow gap, just wide enough for a determined girl to wriggle through. Her heart hammering, she pushed into the leaves and squeezed into the darkness beyond.

For a moment she could see nothing at all. Then her eyes adjusted, and her breath caught in her throat. The garden had not died, as she expected; it had run wild and gloriously free. Roses climbed in great untrimmed arches overhead, and the air was thick with the hum of a thousand drowsy bees. A fountain, long silent, brimmed with rainwater, and around it grew flowers in every colour she could name, and several she could not.

In the centre stood a small stone statue of a girl, her arms outstretched as if to welcome visitors after all these years. Beneath it, almost swallowed by grass, lay a flat carved plaque. Maya knelt and brushed the moss aside with trembling hands. “For Edith,” it read, “who loved this garden best of all.” Maya sat back on her heels, suddenly understanding why the door had been chained, and why nobody ever spoke of it.
She would tell her great-aunt what she had found, she decided. Perhaps, between the two of them, the garden need not stay forgotten any longer.

questions.prompt: How long had Maya been at the house before she found the wall?
questions.explanation: The opening line says Maya had been at the house ‘for three rainy days before she found the wall’.

questions.prompt: What was hanging across the wooden door in the wall?
options: A wooden bar nailed in place.
A heavy iron chain, locked tight.
questions.answer: A heavy iron chain, locked tight.
questions.explanation: The text says ‘across it hung a heavy iron chain, locked tight and red with rust’.

questions.prompt: What did Maya find standing in the centre of the garden?
options: A great untrimmed arch of roses.
A small stone statue of a girl.
questions.answer: A small stone statue of a girl.
questions.explanation: The passage says ‘In the centre stood a small stone statue of a girl, her arms outstretched’.

questions.prompt: How does Maya feel just before she squeezes through the gap in the wall?
questions.explanation: Her ‘heart hammering’ as she pushes into the leaves shows nervous excitement rather than calm or boredom.

questions.prompt: Why does Maya understand ‘why the door had been chained’ after reading the plaque?
options: Because the garden was full of dangerous bees.
Because the plaque shows the garden held a sad, private memory for Edith.
Because the statue was extremely valuable.
Because the fountain might overflow with rainwater.
Because thieves had once broken into the house.
questions.answer: Because the plaque shows the garden held a sad, private memory for Edith.
questions.explanation: The plaque reads ‘For Edith, who loved this garden best of all’, suggesting a personal, painful memory that explains the locked door.

questions.prompt: Which of these statements is TRUE?
options: Maya found the garden dead and bare.
Great-Aunt Edith showed Maya the garden herself.
The garden had run wild and was full of life.
The fountain was still spraying water high into the air.
The door opened easily when Maya pushed it.
questions.answer: The garden had run wild and was full of life.
questions.explanation: The text says the garden ‘had not died’ but ‘had run wild and gloriously free’, alive with roses and bees. The other statements contradict the passage.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "rambling" (line 3)?
questions.explanation: A ‘rambling’ house is one that spreads out in an irregular, sprawling way, with many rooms and corridors.

questions.prompt: Which word is closest in meaning to "determined" (line 23)?
questions.explanation: ‘Determined’ means firmly set on doing something, so ‘resolute’ is the closest match.

questions.prompt: What does the phrase "Curiosity tugged at her like a fish on a line" (line 18) suggest?
options: Maya was frightened and wanted to run away.
Maya felt strongly pulled to find out what was inside.
Maya was tired and wanted to rest by the wall.
Maya was thinking about going fishing.
Maya had completely lost interest in the door.
questions.answer: Maya felt strongly pulled to find out what was inside.
questions.explanation: The image of a fish pulled by a line shows how powerfully her curiosity was drawing her towards the hidden garden.

questions.prompt: In "she pushed into the leaves" (line 24), what type of word is "pushed"?
questions.explanation: ‘Pushed’ describes an action that Maya does, so it is a verb.
